answer:
**Chapter 42** ("De l’influence exercée sur quelques-uns des peuples d’Europe, par les circonstances locales au milieu desquelles ils ont été placés" / "Of the influence exercised on some of the peoples of Europe, by the local circumstances in the midst of which they have been placed") of Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation, Vol. III* contains the core argument that climate and terrain shape productive activity.[^1]

The operative claim appears early in the chapter:

> The nature of the soil and the temperature of the atmosphere have, on all agricultural productions, an influence that it is not necessary to demonstrate; but, in their turn, the products of agriculture exercise on almost all the arts an influence no less extensive.[^1]

Comte illustrates this by contrasting nations based on their soil's capacity for supporting herds or producing fibers like cotton versus those limited to vines, and how navigable waters further enable industry and commerce.[^2] Although Vol. III surveys global populations (including Caucasian/European-stock peoples in Asia and Africa in prior chapters like 30–35), this explicit causal link between physical environment and economic pursuits is developed here for European contexts.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [p-f9e941](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/42-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-quelques-uns-des-peuple/translation/#p-f9e941/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [ch. 42](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/42-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-quelques-uns-des-peuple/translation/).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [work wiki](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/wiki/_work.card.md).
